name: La Comella Loop from Andorra la Vella
name_original: Subida a La Comella — circular desde Andorra la Vella
description: Cycle the moderate 10.4-mile La Comella Loop from Andorra la Vella, gaining 1465 feet with panoramic city and mountain views.
region_intro: No traffic touring cycling routes in the Pyrenees traverse a diverse mountain range characterized by challenging climbs, deep valleys, and high mountain passes. The region features rugged peaks, lush green valleys, and areas with crystal-clear lakes and rushing rivers, offering varied terrain for cyclists. National and regional parks protect vast areas of unspoiled nature, providing numerous opportunities for exploration on two wheels. Best no traffic touring cycling routes in the Pyrenees The most popular no traffic touring cycling route is Betren - Vielha - Es Bòrdes - Gausac - Escunhau — circular through Medio Arán, a 19.4 miles (31.2 km) trail that takes about 2 hours 7 minutes to complete, offering a circular journey through the Medio Arán valley. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Monte Jaizquíbel — circular desde Hondarribia por el Faro de Cabo Higuer, a difficult 24.9 miles (40.1 km) path. This route provides coastal views and challenging ascents around Monte Jaizquíbel. Local touring cyclists also love the Varradòs – cycle touring in Catalonia, a 28.9 miles (46.6 km) trail leading through mountainous terrain with significant elevation gain, often completed in about 4 hours 35 minutes. Touring cycling in the Pyrenees is defined by iconic mountain passes, picturesque valleys, and crystal-clear lakes. The extensive network of routes offers options for different ability levels, from challenging climbs to more gentle paths. The routes in Pyrenees are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 6,900 reviews. More than 200,000 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Pyrenees's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many no-traffic touring cycling routes are available in the Pyrenees?
A: The Pyrenees offer a vast network of routes for touring cyclists seeking traffic-free experiences. On komoot, you can find over 3,000 touring cycling routes, with more than 250 specifically categorized as easy, making them ideal for a relaxed, no-traffic ride.

Q: What is the best time of year for no-traffic touring cycling in the Pyrenees?
A: The best time for no-traffic touring cycling in the Pyrenees is generally from late spring (May) to early autumn (October). During these months, the weather is more stable, mountain passes are typically clear of snow, and the landscapes are vibrant. Summer offers warmer temperatures, while spring and autumn provide cooler rides and fewer crowds.

Q: What do other touring cyclists enjoy most about the no-traffic routes in the Pyrenees?
A: The komoot community highly rates the no-traffic touring cycling routes in the Pyrenees, with an average score of 4.5 out of 5 stars from nearly 7,000 reviews. Cyclists frequently praise the breathtaking mountain scenery, the tranquility of car-free paths, and the diverse terrain that ranges from lush valleys to challenging climbs. The opportunity to discover charming villages and natural wonders like waterfalls and lakes is also a common highlight.

Q: Are there easy no-traffic touring cycling routes suitable for beginners or families in the Pyrenees?
A: Yes, the Pyrenees offer several easy no-traffic touring cycling routes perfect for beginners and families. These routes often follow dedicated Greenways or Vias Verdes, providing flat or gently undulating terrain away from vehicle traffic. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e860410706/argeles-gazost-and-gaves-greenway-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Argelès-Gazost and Gaves Greenway Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which offers a pleasant ride through scenic landscapes.

Q: Can I find circular no-traffic touring cycling routes in the Pyrenees?
A: Absolutely. Many no-traffic touring cycling routes in the Pyrenees are designed as circular loops, allowing you to start and end your journey at the same point. This is convenient for those traveling by car. For instance, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e967362332/betren-vielha-es-bordes-gausac-escunhau-circular-through-medio-aran" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Betren - Vielha - Es Bòrdes - Gausac - Escunhau — circular through Medio Arán&lt;/a&gt; route, which offers a diverse loop through the Medio Arán region.

Q: What natural attractions can I expect to see along no-traffic touring cycling routes in the Pyrenees?
A: The Pyrenees are rich in natural beauty. Along no-traffic routes, you can encounter stunning features such as crystal-clear lakes, impressive waterfalls, and dramatic mountain passes. Highlights include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5041014" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Chorro Waterfall&lt;/a&gt;, the serene &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e863014627/discover-the-beautiful-lake-of-estaing" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lake of Estaing&lt;/a&gt;, and iconic mountain passes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/128883" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Col du Soulor&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/128884" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Col d'Aspin&lt;/a&gt;, offering breathtaking views.

Q: Are there any historical or cultural sites accessible from no-traffic cycling routes?
A: Yes, the Pyrenees are dotted with charming villages and historical sites that can be explored from no-traffic cycling routes. You might pass through medieval towns, discover ancient caves like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2204863" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Grotte du Mas-d'Azil&lt;/a&gt;, or visit unique religious sites such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3065940" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Juan Xar Cave Shrine&lt;/a&gt;. These routes often provide a peaceful way to immerse yourself in the region's rich cultural heritage.

Q: How can I find parking near the starting points of no-traffic touring cycling routes?
A: Many popular no-traffic touring cycling routes in the Pyrenees, especially those on Greenways or Vias Verdes, have designated parking areas at their trailheads or in nearby towns. It's advisable to check the specific route details on komoot, as users often provide information about parking availability in the route descriptions or comments. Larger towns like Hondarribia, a starting point for the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1064123551/monte-jaizquibel-circular-desde-hondarribia-por-el-faro-de-cabo-higuer" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Monte Jaizquíbel circular route&lt;/a&gt;, typically offer ample parking options.

Q: Are dogs allowed on no-traffic touring cycling routes in the Pyrenees?
A: Policies regarding dogs on cycling routes can vary depending on the specific trail or protected area. While many general paths are dog-friendly, some national or regional parks may require dogs to be on a leash or restrict access in certain zones to protect wildlife. Always check local regulations for the specific route you plan to cycle to ensure a pleasant experience for both you and your dog.

Q: Can I access no-traffic touring cycling routes using public transport?
A: Accessing no-traffic touring cycling routes via public transport in the Pyrenees is possible, though it requires some planning. Regional trains and buses often serve towns and villages near popular cycling paths. Some services may accommodate bicycles, but it's crucial to verify this in advance, especially during peak season. Consider routes that start or end in larger towns with better public transport connections for easier access.

Q: Are there any less crowded no-traffic cycling options for a more peaceful experience?
A: For a more peaceful and less crowded experience, consider exploring routes in lesser-known valleys or during the shoulder seasons (late spring or early autumn). While popular Greenways can attract more visitors, many of the Pyrenees' no-traffic routes traverse remote and tranquil landscapes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1285597444/varrados-cycle-touring-in-catalonia" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Varradòs – cycle touring in Catalonia&lt;/a&gt; route, for example, offers a challenging yet serene journey through the Catalan Pyrenees.
tips:
The Church of Saint Stephen[1]​
(in Catalan: Església de Sant Esteve) is a church located in the Príncep Benlloch square in Andorra la Vella, the capital of the principality of Andorra. It is a protected property registered in the Cultural Heritage of Andorra. It was built in the 12th century and then restored in the 20th century.
Originally Romanesque, it has been modified and enlarged on different occasions, the last and most important in the 20th century.
The Romanesque semicircular apse (13th century), the largest in Andorra, and the start of the nave walls have been preserved from the original work. On the outside, the apse presents a decoration made with a frieze and with arches and Lombard bands. The bell tower, with a square plan, is a slender three-story tower, without
Short and hard port that serves as a "round" to cross Andorra la Vella, 4.3 km at 7.9%, and the possibility of connecting with the road to the beautiful lake of Engolasters.
Beautiful views from the top of this port, short, but hard and that avoids us having to cross the entire urban center of Andorra la Vella

faqs.question: How difficult is the La Comella Loop from Andorra la Vella for touring cyclists?
faqs.answer: The La Comella Loop is generally considered a moderate challenge for touring cyclists. While the ascent to La Comella features a significant climb with an average gradient of 8.1% over 4.3 km, the main road sections are on smooth asphalt. However, some variants of the loop can include unpaved and difficult segments, so it's important to check the specific route you plan to take.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The terrain is varied. The primary ascent to La Comella is on smooth asphalt roads, offering a consistent slope. However, depending on the specific loop variant, you might encounter unpaved sections, forest trails with roots, and loose stones, which are more suited for mountain bikes or e-bikes. For road-oriented touring bikes, focus on the predominantly paved routes.

faqs.question: What are the key viewpoints and landmarks along the La Comella Loop?
faqs.answer: As you ascend, you'll find magnificent views. Notable spots include lookout points like the Bosc Negre, offering spectacular views of the surrounding mountains and valley. Upon reaching Port de la Comella, you'll be rewarded with panoramic views over Andorra la Vella. The route also passes near the Church of Saint Stephen, Andorra la Vella.

faqs.question: Where can I park and access the trailhead for this route?
faqs.answer: The route typically starts from Andorra la Vella itself. As it's a loop originating from the city, you can usually find parking within Andorra la Vella. There isn't a specific 'trailhead' parking lot in the traditional sense for this urban-starting cycling route; you would begin your ride from a convenient point in the city.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle the La Comella Loop?
faqs.answer: Given Andorra's mountainous climate, the best time to cycle this loop is generally from late spring through early autumn (May to October). During these months, the weather is typically more favorable, and the roads are clear of snow and ice, providing the most enjoyable and safest cycling conditions.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for this cycling route?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle the La Comella Loop from Andorra la Vella. The roads and trails used are generally open to the public.

faqs.question: Is the La Comella Loop suitable for beginner cyclists?
faqs.answer: Due to the significant elevation gain and the challenging 8.1% average gradient on the main ascent, this route is generally not recommended for beginner cyclists. It's best suited for intermediate cyclists with good fitness who are comfortable with sustained climbs.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other well-known cycling or hiking paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route intersects with several other paths. Notably, it covers significant portions of 'El Camí IT1TR5 El Camí Capçaler: de Salzes a Balaguer' (42%), '[CIMA AN04] La Comella' (27%), and 'GR 7 - Andorra' (26%). You'll also find smaller sections of other local trails.

faqs.question: Are there any places to stop for a break or refreshments along the way?
faqs.answer: While the route itself doesn't feature many dedicated cafes directly on the climb, the Bosc Negre lookout point is mentioned as a good spot for a break with spectacular views. You'll find plenty of cafes and restaurants in Andorra la Vella before and after your ride.

faqs.question: Is the La Comella Loop from Andorra la Vella dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: This route is primarily a touring bicycle route on paved roads and some potentially unpaved sections. While dogs are generally welcome in many outdoor areas in Andorra, bringing a dog along on a bicycle tour of this nature would depend on your specific setup (e.g., a bike trailer) and the dog's comfort. If you plan to walk sections, standard leash rules would apply.

faqs.question: What makes the La Comella ascent notable for cyclists?
faqs.answer: The La Comella ascent is particularly notable for its inclusion in major professional cycling events like the Vuelta a España and the Tour de France over the past 30 years. This highlights its quality as a challenging and scenic climb with smooth asphalt, making it a favorite among serious road cyclists.

waypoints.name: Church of Saint Stephen, Andorra la Vella
